etymology.notes: ʕrǧ ‘lahm sein, lahmen, hinken, humpeln’ Wehr 826

Infectum.turoyo: gd-əṯyo emo d-aryo raġla qəṣto-yo ko-mhalxo ko-mʕaržo me-raġla
translations:
‘Then a mother lion shall come, her foot is swollen. She limps
			on her foot (while) she walks’ 75/283;

29.
			I=bruske nayiḥo. Eló balé ṣaqaṭ yo. Ṣaqaṭ yo, komaʕrəǧ
			b ḥḏo raġlo.

The
			pain receded. But he became lame, we was limping with one of his
			legs (Sultan_Nisane)
